content.content:
1	\section{The Dispersion and Scattering Budget}
2	\label{sec:budget}
3	
4	% Core methods section. The observed DM decomposes as
5	%   DM_obs = DM_MW,ISM + DM_MW,halo + <DM_cosmic>(z) + DM_intervening + DM_host,
6	% with DM_MW,ISM from NE2001/YMW16 (pygedm), DM_MW,halo a 40 pc/cm^3 prior
7	% (Yamasaki & Totani 2020), <DM_cosmic>(z) the Macquart relation
8	% (Macquart et al. 2020; f_IGM=0.84, chi_e=0.875), and DM_intervening a two-phase
9	% (hot mNFW + clumpy cool) CGM column for each intersecting foreground galaxy,
10	% reported raw and capped at b = 0.1 R_vir (the galaxy-interior regime).
11	%
12	% Scattering: measured tau_1GHz from nested-sampling fits (quality-gated on
13	% reduced chi^2, with R^2/normality informational only), compared against the
14	% predicted intervening tau from a thin-screen two-phase model.
15	
16	\subsection{Dispersion Measure Decomposition}
17	\label{sec:dm}
18	
19	The observed dispersion measure of each co-detection separates into Galactic,
20	cosmological, host, and (where the sightline intersects a foreground galaxy)
21	intervening contributions \citep{CordesChatterjee2019},
22	\begin{equation}
23	\mathrm{DM_{obs}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW}} + \langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)
24	+ \mathrm{DM_{int}} + \mathrm{DM_{host}},
25	\label{eq:dmbudget}
26	\end{equation}
27	with the Galactic term $\mathrm{DM_{MW}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}} +
28	\mathrm{DM_{MW,halo}}$ split between disk and halo. We estimate the disk
29	contribution $\mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}}$ from the NE2001 \citep{CordesLazio2002} and
30	YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} electron-density models and adopt a
31	$\sim$40\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ Milky-Way halo prior \citep{YamasakiTotani2020}. The
32	cosmological mean $\langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)$ follows the Macquart
33	relation \citep{Macquart2020}. For the sightlines that cross a foreground
34	galaxy we model the intervening circumgalactic column $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ as a
35	two-phase (hot modified-NFW plus clumpy cool) medium, taking the mNFW gas
36	profile from \citet{ProchaskaZheng2019}. Such intervening halos can imprint
37	measurable dispersion and, where the gas is turbulent, scattering on FRB
38	sightlines \citep{Ocker2021}, although an individual quiescent halo may instead
39	yield only an upper limit on its scattering contribution \citep{Prochaska2019}.
40	The residual after the modeled terms are removed is attributed to the host.
41	
42	\subsection{Intervening Foreground Galaxies and Clusters}
43	\label{sec:foreground}
44	
45	The intervening term $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ is sourced only where a sightline
46	actually intersects foreground circumgalactic or intracluster gas, so we first
47	establish which candidate systems along the twelve sightlines are genuinely in
48	the foreground. Each candidate is cross-matched against the DESI Legacy Imaging
49	Survey DR9 with \citet{Zhou2021} photometric redshifts, DESI DR1 spectroscopic
50	redshifts, the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED), and PS1-STRM
51	\citep{Beck2021}---by coordinate match (morphology and redshift) and, for the
52	PS1-classified halos, by direct \texttt{objID} lookup. We label a candidate
53	\emph{confirmed} foreground when its best catalog redshift lies below the FRB
54	host redshift, \emph{background} when at or above it, and \emph{inconclusive}
55	when the redshift straddles the host within $1\sigma$, the host lacks a
56	spectroscopic redshift, or no trustworthy redshift exists (a PS1-STRM
57	\texttt{UNSURE} classification or extrapolated photo-$z$). Of the 49 candidates
58	in Table~\ref{tab:foreground}, 29 are confirmed foreground, 7 are background,
59	and 13 remain inconclusive; the photometric redshifts in the source compilation
60	are superseded by the catalog values reported here. Only one of the 15 clusters
61	has an impact parameter inside its own $R_{500}$, so for the rest the sightline
62	does not pierce the intracluster medium and the cluster contributes negligibly
63	to $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$.
64	
65	\input{foreground_table.tex}
66	
67	\subsection{Scattering Attribution}
68	\label{sec:scattering}
69	
70	We measure the pulse-broadening time and its frequency scaling from the
71	co-detected dynamic spectra and compare them against the scattering predicted
72	by each candidate screen: the Milky-Way ISM, an intervening circumgalactic
73	medium, or a host galaxy, where the host can dominate the scattering of a
74	heavily broadened burst \citep{Ocker2022}. The fits are produced by the
75	\texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline (\texttt{pipeline/}).
76	
77	\begin{figure}
78	  \centering
79	  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{wilhelm_scint_dnud_ne2025}
80	  \caption{Scintillation decorrelation bandwidth $\Delta\nu_d$ versus frequency for
81	  FRB~20221203A. DSA-resolved $\Delta\nu_d\approx0.13$\,MHz (blue; nearly flat,
82	  $x=-0.23\pm0.19$ over the 10\% DSA lever arm); CHIME upper limits (red) since the
83	  diffractive scale is sub-channel at native resolution. Purple stars: the NE2025
84	  Milky-Way scattering floor, $\sim8\times$ \emph{above} the measurement. Green
85	  dash-dot: the pulse-broadening screen ($2\pi\tau\,\Delta\nu_d=C_1=1$), $\sim85\times$
86	  \emph{below}. The three separated scales imply the dominant pulse-broadening screen
87	  is neither the Galactic floor nor the resolved scintillation screen (a strong
88	  host/intervening screen). NE2025 Galactic electron-density model from
89	  \citet{Ocker2026}.}
90	  \label{fig:scint_screens}
91	\end{figure}
92	
93	\begin{figure}
94	  \centering
95	  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{codetection_scint_excess}
96	  \caption{Is the FRB 20221203A scattering excess sightline-specific or systematic?
97	  Scattering excess (NE2025 Milky-Way-floor $\Delta\nu_d$ divided by the measured
98	  DSA diffractive $\Delta\nu_d$; excess${=}1$ is the MW floor, dashed) versus
99	  Galactic latitude $|b|$ for the 12 CHIME--DSA co-detections. Error bars combine
100	  the measurement error with NE2025's floor uncertainty ($\sigma\approx0.4$\,dex,
101	  $\sim\!\times2.5$); the annotated $z$ is the significance above the MW floor.
102	  $\Delta\nu_d$ is the narrowest consistent cluster of well-constrained
103	  Lorentzian/Gen-Lorentz widths across all stored per-subband scintillation fits
104	  (rejecting fits railed at the $0.060$\,MHz bound). Filled circles: cleanly-resolved
105	  diffractive scales significant at $z>2$ -- FRB 20220207C ($10.7\times$), FRB 20221203A
106	  ($8.0\times$), FRB 20230913A ($7.2\times$), all in the shaded band. Open circle: FRB 20240229A
107	  ($9.6\times$, single-subband, low confidence). Light-blue: clean but $z<2$
108	  (FRB 20240203A $5.9\times$, FRB 20220506D $2.6\times$). Orange up-triangles: lower limits
109	  (single-component or rail-only fits). The grey band ($|b|<14^\circ$) marks
110	  sightlines where NE2025 intersects voids and the floor is unreliable (FRB 20230814B,
111	  FRB 20221113A, FRB 20240122A fall below unity there). The excess clusters at $\sim7$--$11\times$ on
112	  $3$--$4$ independent mid-$|b|$ sightlines where the floor is reliable and the
113	  diffractive scale resolvable (three high-confidence: FRB 20220207C, FRB 20221203A, FRB 20230913A;
114	  FRB 20240229A adds a fourth at high $|b|$ but is low-confidence), and never the reverse --
115	  evidence for a common (host/intervening) scattering enhancement rather than a
116	  FRB 20221203A-only peculiarity. Per-sightline significance is $\sim\!2\sigma$, dominated
117	  by NE2025's floor uncertainty; because that error is a shared systematic across
118	  sightlines (not independent), the result rests on the \emph{asymmetry} -- the
119	  measurement is always in excess where the floor is reliable, never below -- rather
120	  than on a stacked significance. NE2025 Galactic electron-density model from
121	  \citet{Ocker2026}.}
122	  \label{fig:codetection_excess}
123	\end{figure}
124	
125	Figure~\ref{fig:scint_screens} shows the three separated scattering scales for the
126	prototype sightline FRB~20221203A, and Figure~\ref{fig:codetection_excess} extends the
127	Milky-Way-floor comparison to all twelve co-detections. The diffractive-scintillation
128	excess relative to the modeled Milky-Way floor is robust to the choice of Galactic
129	electron-density model among the scattering-calibrated models of the same lineage:
130	NE2025 \citep{Ocker2026} yields an excess on all six mid-latitude sightlines
131	($2.6$--$10.7\times$) and NE2001 \citep{CordesLazio2002} confirms it ($6/6$,
132	$2.2$--$7.3\times$), while YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} reverses it, predicting a floor below the
133	measured value on five of the six. We attribute this reversal to YMW16's per-sightline
134	scattering unreliability rather than to an absence of excess: by construction YMW16
135	incorporates no interstellar-scattering data and derives $\tau$ from an empirical
136	$\tau$--$\mathrm{DM}$ relation with $\sim\!1$\,dex scatter, whereas NE2025 and NE2001
137	forward-model scattering from the electron-density-fluctuation field. Because the adopted
138	NE2025 floor itself carries a factor $\sim\!2$--$3$ per-sightline uncertainty that acts as a
139	systematic shared across sightlines---and therefore does not average down as $\sqrt{N}$---we
140	regard only the high-amplitude sightlines ($\sim\!7$--$11\times$) as robust evidence for an
141	extragalactic scattering screen, with the lowest-amplitude cases ($\sim\!2.6$--$3\times$)
142	remaining consistent with the floor uncertainty.
143	
144	\subsubsection{The joint two-band fit}
145	\label{sec:jointfit}
146	
147	A single-band scattering fit cannot separate the scattering index $\alpha$ from
148	the scattering time, because the pulse broadening
149	$\tau(\nu)=\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,(\nu/1\,\mathrm{GHz})^{-\alpha}$ enters a
150	single band only through its value at that band's frequency: one band fixes the
151	product, not the slope. Co-detection breaks this degeneracy. We fit the
152	CHIME/FRB ($\sim$0.4--0.8\,GHz) and DSA-110 ($\sim$1.4\,GHz) dynamic spectra
153	\emph{simultaneously} with a shared sightline pair
154	$(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ and per-telescope pulse parameters (arrival
155	time $t_0$, intrinsic width $\zeta$, and a residual dispersion
156	$\delta\mathrm{DM}$). The $\sim$1\,GHz lever arm between the bands measures
157	$\alpha$ directly: the ratio of the two scattering times pins the slope, while
158	the shared normalization $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ pins its value at 1\,GHz. With
159	$\alpha$ held at the Kolmogorov value $4.4$ \citep{Rickett1990}, the two bands
160	instead return $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ estimates that disagree by up to an
161	order of magnitude for the same sightline---evidence that $\alpha\neq4.4$ along
162	these lines of sight.
163	
164	The shape of the scattering tail---the pulse-broadening function (PBF)---is set
165	by the geometry of the scattering medium: a single thin screen produces an
166	exponential tail, while an extended or shallow-spectrum medium produces a heavier
167	power-law tail (Figure~\ref{fig:pbf_shapes}; \citealp[\S11.2]{Cordes2025}). Fit
168	band by band, individual bursts can appear to prefer different PBFs in the two
169	bands---for FRB~20221203A a Kolmogorov power-law improves the CHIME evidence while
170	DSA-110 rejects any tail heavier than exponential (Figure~\ref{fig:pbf_evidence}),
171	and a per-band PBF formally beats a single exponential by $\Delta\ln Z\approx+4$.
172	A single sightline, however, samples one scattering screen, whose PBF \emph{shape}
173	is fixed by its geometry; only the timescale $\tau$ scales with frequency. A PBF
174	that switches functional form between $0.6$ and $1.4$\,GHz is therefore
175	unphysical, and the per-band preference is better read as the fit absorbing
176	band-specific profile structure---an unmodeled sub-component or intrinsic
177	asymmetry---into the tail shape. We accordingly adopt a single exponential PBF for
178	both bands. Re-fitting the full sample under this physical choice moves the
179	well-constrained scattering indices by $\le0.1$ relative to the per-band fits---so
180	the simplification costs nothing for the clean sightlines---while a marginal,
181	poorly-constrained sightline shifts by ${\approx}0.6$ in $\alpha$
182	(Figure~\ref{fig:alpha_pbf}), confirming that the PBF must be fixed on physical
183	grounds before a per-sightline index is quoted.
184	
185	\begin{figure}
186	  \centering
187	  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{pbf_shapes}
188	  \caption{Pulse-broadening function (PBF) shapes used in the joint fit. A Gaussian
189	  intrinsic pulse convolved with a thin-screen exponential PBF (green) versus
190	  power-law-spectrum PBFs with density index $\beta=3.00$ and $\beta=11/3$
191	  (Kolmogorov). Smaller $\beta$ yields a heavier $t^{-\beta/2}$ tail; $\beta\to4$
192	  recovers the exponential \citep[\S11.2]{Cordes2025}.}
193	  \label{fig:pbf_shapes}
194	\end{figure}
195	
196	\begin{figure*}
197	  \centering
198	  \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{wilhelm_pbf_evidence}
199	  \caption{Band-dependent PBF preference for FRB~20221203A. \emph{Left:} change in
200	  log-evidence relative to the exponential PBF versus power-law index $\beta$ (best
201	  single-component, per band). CHIME (400--800\,MHz) mildly prefers a Kolmogorov
202	  tail ($\Delta\ln Z=+6.2$ at $\beta=11/3$); DSA (1.2--1.5\,GHz) rejects
203	  heavier-than-exponential tails monotonically. \emph{Right:} joint CHIME+DSA
204	  evidence (C1D1). A \emph{per-band} PBF (CHIME power-law, DSA exponential) beats the
205	  all-exponential model by $\Delta\ln Z=+4.0$ ($\sim6\sigma$), whereas a global
206	  power-law loses $84$. Taken at face value this favors a per-band PBF; we argue
207	  in the text that it is an unphysical artifact and adopt a single exponential PBF.}
208	  \label{fig:pbf_evidence}
209	\end{figure*}
210	
211	\begin{figure}
212	  \centering
213	  \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{alpha_pbf_systematic}
214	  \caption{Effect of the PBF choice on the per-sightline scattering index. For
215	  each burst the open marker is $\alpha$ under the (unphysical) per-band PBF and
216	  the filled marker, with $1\sigma$ posterior, is $\alpha$ under the adopted
217	  single-exponential PBF. The well-constrained single-screen sightlines (green)
218	  are insensitive to the choice ($|\Delta\alpha|\le0.1$); only a marginal,
219	  poorly-constrained sightline (orange) moves appreciably (${\approx}0.6$), and
220	  two sightlines are pinned at the $\alpha=1.5$ prior floor under either PBF
221	  (grey). The dashed line marks the Kolmogorov value $\alpha=4.4$.}
222	  \label{fig:alpha_pbf}
223	\end{figure}
224	
225	A complementary, model-free check that the recovered index is not corrupted by
226	unmodelled multiplicity is to resolve each burst's profile \emph{within} the CHIME
227	band. Splitting the band into four $80\,\mathrm{MHz}$ sub-bands and tracking the
228	on-pulse width with frequency separates genuine scattering, whose width grows as
229	$\nu^{-\alpha}$, from frequency-independent intrinsic sub-structure, which does not
230	(Figure~\ref{fig:chime_subband_compare}); a burst whose sub-band widths fail to
231	scale is a candidate for hidden sub-components and is flagged before a single-screen
232	index is quoted.
233	
234	\begin{figure}
235	  \centering
236	  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{chime_subband_compare}
237	  \caption{Within-CHIME sub-band profiles for the co-detected FRBs johndoeII and
238	  wilhelm, each split into four $80\,\mathrm{MHz}$ sub-bands across
239	  $0.40$--$0.80\,\mathrm{GHz}$. Each panel shows the baseline-subtracted on-pulse
240	  profile against time, annotated with the model-free rms width $w_{\mathrm{rms}}$
241	  and the trailing-edge $e$-folding time $w_{\mathrm{tail}}$. A purely scattering
242	  origin predicts both widths increasing toward lower frequency as $\nu^{-\alpha}$,
243	  whereas frequency-independent intrinsic sub-structure does not; the empty
244	  $0.40$--$0.48\,\mathrm{GHz}$ sub-band for wilhelm is a data gap, not a
245	  non-detection. This profile-shape evolution is the diagnostic used to flag
246	  unmodelled temporal sub-components before a scattering index is quoted. Generated
247	  by the \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline.}
248	  \label{fig:chime_subband_compare}
249	\end{figure}
250	
251	A stronger, quantitative version of this check fits the scattering time directly
252	in each sub-band. We split each band into sub-bands (four across CHIME, three
253	across DSA-110), collapse each to a one-dimensional profile, and fit a scattered
254	pulse---a Gaussian intrinsic shape convolved with a one-sided exponential tail,
255	the exponentially-modified Gaussian---to each sub-band independently, leaving the
256	scattering time $\tau$ free at every frequency. Because each $\tau_i$ is measured
257	per sub-band with no power law imposed, the slope of $\tau_i(\nu_i)$ is an estimate
258	of $\alpha$ that shares neither parametrization nor likelihood with the joint
259	two-dimensional fit, so its agreement with the joint value is an independent
260	validation rather than a self-consistency check
261	(Figure~\ref{fig:subband_tau}). For the well-constrained single-screen
262	sightlines the sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ trace the joint power law and recover the joint
263	index to within $1.6\sigma$ (e.g.\ FRB~20240229A, $\alpha=2.40$ versus
264	$2.35\pm0.03$; FRB~20221203A, $2.56$ versus $2.45\pm0.07$). The check also
265	localizes the one elevated band $\chi^2$ in our sample: the DSA profile of
266	FRB~20221203A is imperfectly fit, but its sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ still follow the
267	joint slope, so the residual reflects unmodeled profile sub-structure rather than a
268	failure of the single-screen scattering law, and $\alpha$ is unaffected. The method
269	has a built-in horizon: where a burst is faint and heavily scattered the per-sub-band
270	fit cannot resolve the broad CHIME tail, and at the top of the DSA band $\tau$ falls
271	below the time resolution, so the most strongly scattered sightlines (e.g.\
272	FRB~20220506D) retain only DSA detections over too short a lever to constrain
273	$\alpha$ on their own---there the joint fit's cross-band lever does work that no
274	single band can. We therefore use the sub-band reconstruction as a corroborating
275	cross-check on the well-constrained indices, not as a primary estimator.
276	
277	\begin{figure*}
278	  \centering
279	  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{subband_tau_validation_grid}
280	  \caption{Independent sub-band validation of the joint scattering index. For each
281	  burst the scattering time $\tau$ is fit directly in four CHIME (blue) and three
282	  DSA-110 (red) sub-bands with $\tau$ free at every frequency, and compared to the
283	  joint-fit power law $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,\nu^{-\alpha}$ (black); each panel
284	  lists the joint $\alpha$ and the slope $\alpha$ of the independent sub-band points.
285	  \emph{Top:} three well-constrained sightlines whose sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ recover
286	  the joint index to within $1.6\sigma$. \emph{Bottom:} two sightlines with a mild
287	  ($\Delta\alpha\approx0.4$) offset traceable to the intrinsic-width--scattering
288	  degeneracy in a single bright band (FRB~20230325A) and to a low DSA $\tau$
289	  (FRB~20230307A), and one strongly-scattered sightline (FRB~20220506D) whose CHIME
290	  tail is unresolved, leaving only DSA detections over too short a lever to constrain
291	  $\alpha$. Downward triangles are $2\sigma$ upper limits; sub-bands where the
292	  scattered-pulse model does not describe the profile are dropped rather than shown
293	  as limits. Generated by the \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline.}
294	  \label{fig:subband_tau}
295	\end{figure*}
296	
297	\subsubsection{Gain-marginalized likelihood}
298	\label{sec:gainmarg}
299	
300	The per-channel amplitude of an FRB spectrum is unknown and strongly modulated
301	by scintillation; carried as a free spectral envelope it leaves correlated
302	structure in the fit residuals and invalidates a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test.
303	We therefore marginalize the per-channel gain analytically. With a Gaussian
304	prior on the gain the per-channel evidence is available in closed form (a
305	matched-filter likelihood), and the recovered gain absorbs both the burst
306	spectrum and the scintillation pattern. This removes the spectral and
307	scintillation structure from the residual, so that the reduced $\chi^2$ and the
308	lag-1 residual autocorrelation become meaningful quality gates, and it lowers
309	the sampled dimensionality. We
310	sample the posterior by nested sampling \citep{Skilling2004}, as implemented in
311	\texttt{dynesty} \citep{Speagle2020}, and quality-gate each fit on its reduced
312	$\chi^2$ and residual whiteness, treating $R^2$ and residual normality as
313	informational.
314	
315	\subsubsection{Multiple temporal components}
316	\label{sec:multicomp}
317	
318	Some bursts comprise more than one temporal sub-component, a common feature of
319	the resolved FRB population \citep{Hessels2019, Pleunis2021}. An unmodeled
320	second component is partially absorbed by a single scattered pulse---as a steeper slope
321	and a larger scattering time---and so biases the inferred $\alpha$. We extend
322	the likelihood to $N$ temporal components per band, each contributing its own
323	$(t_0,\zeta)$ while $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ remain shared across both
324	bands and all components, and we select the number of components per band by
325	Bayesian evidence. Because the evidence normalization differs between likelihood
326	variants, the component counts are compared on a single matched grid in which
327	even the one-component model is evaluated through the multi-component
328	likelihood, so that evidence differences are meaningful. Component arrival times
329	within a band are ordered and separated by a data-derived minimum spacing,
330	keeping the fit identifiable at every prior sample. A clean single-component
331	co-detection in our sample, for which the joint fit returns
332	$\alpha=4.48\pm0.04$ with white residuals, validates the method; a
333	multi-component case is presented in Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}.
334	% Validating clean case = burst internally labelled "FRB 20230325A" (swap to TNS name).
335

\section{The Dispersion and Scattering Budget}
\label{sec:budget}

% Core methods section. The observed DM decomposes as
%   DM_obs = DM_MW,ISM + DM_MW,halo + <DM_cosmic>(z) + DM_intervening + DM_host,
% with DM_MW,ISM from NE2001/YMW16 (pygedm), DM_MW,halo a 40 pc/cm^3 prior
% (Yamasaki & Totani 2020), <DM_cosmic>(z) the Macquart relation
% (Macquart et al. 2020; f_IGM=0.84, chi_e=0.875), and DM_intervening a two-phase
% (hot mNFW + clumpy cool) CGM column for each intersecting foreground galaxy,
% reported raw and capped at b = 0.1 R_vir (the galaxy-interior regime).
%
% Scattering: measured tau_1GHz from nested-sampling fits (quality-gated on
% reduced chi^2, with R^2/normality informational only), compared against the
% predicted intervening tau from a thin-screen two-phase model.

\subsection{Dispersion Measure Decomposition}
\label{sec:dm}

The observed dispersion measure of each co-detection separates into Galactic,
cosmological, host, and (where the sightline intersects a foreground galaxy)
intervening contributions \citep{CordesChatterjee2019},
\begin{equation}
\mathrm{DM_{obs}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW}} + \langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)
+ \mathrm{DM_{int}} + \mathrm{DM_{host}},
\label{eq:dmbudget}
\end{equation}
with the Galactic term $\mathrm{DM_{MW}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}} +
\mathrm{DM_{MW,halo}}$ split between disk and halo. We estimate the disk
contribution $\mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}}$ from the NE2001 \citep{CordesLazio2002} and
YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} electron-density models and adopt a
$\sim$40\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ Milky-Way halo prior \citep{YamasakiTotani2020}. The
cosmological mean $\langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)$ follows the Macquart
relation \citep{Macquart2020}. For the sightlines that cross a foreground
galaxy we model the intervening circumgalactic column $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ as a
two-phase (hot modified-NFW plus clumpy cool) medium, taking the mNFW gas
profile from \citet{ProchaskaZheng2019}. Such intervening halos can imprint
measurable dispersion and, where the gas is turbulent, scattering on FRB
sightlines \citep{Ocker2021}, although an individual quiescent halo may instead
yield only an upper limit on its scattering contribution \citep{Prochaska2019}.
The residual after the modeled terms are removed is attributed to the host.

\subsection{Intervening Foreground Galaxies and Clusters}
\label{sec:foreground}

The intervening term $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ is sourced only where a sightline
actually intersects foreground circumgalactic or intracluster gas, so we first
establish which candidate systems along the twelve sightlines are genuinely in
the foreground. Each candidate is cross-matched against the DESI Legacy Imaging
Survey DR9 with \citet{Zhou2021} photometric redshifts, DESI DR1 spectroscopic
redshifts, the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED), and PS1-STRM
\citep{Beck2021}---by coordinate match (morphology and redshift) and, for the
PS1-classified halos, by direct \texttt{objID} lookup. We label a candidate
\emph{confirmed} foreground when its best catalog redshift lies below the FRB
host redshift, \emph{background} when at or above it, and \emph{inconclusive}
when the redshift straddles the host within $1\sigma$, the host lacks a
spectroscopic redshift, or no trustworthy redshift exists (a PS1-STRM
\texttt{UNSURE} classification or extrapolated photo-$z$). Of the 49 candidates
in Table~\ref{tab:foreground}, 29 are confirmed foreground, 7 are background,
and 13 remain inconclusive; the photometric redshifts in the source compilation
are superseded by the catalog values reported here. Only one of the 15 clusters
has an impact parameter inside its own $R_{500}$, so for the rest the sightline
does not pierce the intracluster medium and the cluster contributes negligibly
to $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$.

\input{foreground_table.tex}

\subsection{Scattering Attribution}
\label{sec:scattering}

We measure the pulse-broadening time and its frequency scaling from the
co-detected dynamic spectra and compare them against the scattering predicted
by each candidate screen: the Milky-Way ISM, an intervening circumgalactic
medium, or a host galaxy, where the host can dominate the scattering of a
heavily broadened burst \citep{Ocker2022}. The fits are produced by the
\texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline (\texttt{pipeline/}).

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{wilhelm_scint_dnud_ne2025}
  \caption{Scintillation decorrelation bandwidth $\Delta\nu_d$ versus frequency for
  FRB~20221203A. DSA-resolved $\Delta\nu_d\approx0.13$\,MHz (blue; nearly flat,
  $x=-0.23\pm0.19$ over the 10\% DSA lever arm); CHIME upper limits (red) since the
  diffractive scale is sub-channel at native resolution. Purple stars: the NE2025
  Milky-Way scattering floor, $\sim8\times$ \emph{above} the measurement. Green
  dash-dot: the pulse-broadening screen ($2\pi\tau\,\Delta\nu_d=C_1=1$), $\sim85\times$
  \emph{below}. The three separated scales imply the dominant pulse-broadening screen
  is neither the Galactic floor nor the resolved scintillation screen (a strong
  host/intervening screen). NE2025 Galactic electron-density model from
  \citet{Ocker2026}.}
  \label{fig:scint_screens}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{codetection_scint_excess}
  \caption{Is the FRB 20221203A scattering excess sightline-specific or systematic?
  Scattering excess (NE2025 Milky-Way-floor $\Delta\nu_d$ divided by the measured
  DSA diffractive $\Delta\nu_d$; excess${=}1$ is the MW floor, dashed) versus
  Galactic latitude $|b|$ for the 12 CHIME--DSA co-detections. Error bars combine
  the measurement error with NE2025's floor uncertainty ($\sigma\approx0.4$\,dex,
  $\sim\!\times2.5$); the annotated $z$ is the significance above the MW floor.
  $\Delta\nu_d$ is the narrowest consistent cluster of well-constrained
  Lorentzian/Gen-Lorentz widths across all stored per-subband scintillation fits
  (rejecting fits railed at the $0.060$\,MHz bound). Filled circles: cleanly-resolved
  diffractive scales significant at $z>2$ -- FRB 20220207C ($10.7\times$), FRB 20221203A
  ($8.0\times$), FRB 20230913A ($7.2\times$), all in the shaded band. Open circle: FRB 20240229A
  ($9.6\times$, single-subband, low confidence). Light-blue: clean but $z<2$
  (FRB 20240203A $5.9\times$, FRB 20220506D $2.6\times$). Orange up-triangles: lower limits
  (single-component or rail-only fits). The grey band ($|b|<14^\circ$) marks
  sightlines where NE2025 intersects voids and the floor is unreliable (FRB 20230814B,
  FRB 20221113A, FRB 20240122A fall below unity there). The excess clusters at $\sim7$--$11\times$ on
  $3$--$4$ independent mid-$|b|$ sightlines where the floor is reliable and the
  diffractive scale resolvable (three high-confidence: FRB 20220207C, FRB 20221203A, FRB 20230913A;
  FRB 20240229A adds a fourth at high $|b|$ but is low-confidence), and never the reverse --
  evidence for a common (host/intervening) scattering enhancement rather than a
  FRB 20221203A-only peculiarity. Per-sightline significance is $\sim\!2\sigma$, dominated
  by NE2025's floor uncertainty; because that error is a shared systematic across
  sightlines (not independent), the result rests on the \emph{asymmetry} -- the
  measurement is always in excess where the floor is reliable, never below -- rather
  than on a stacked significance. NE2025 Galactic electron-density model from
  \citet{Ocker2026}.}
  \label{fig:codetection_excess}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:scint_screens} shows the three separated scattering scales for the
prototype sightline FRB~20221203A, and Figure~\ref{fig:codetection_excess} extends the
Milky-Way-floor comparison to all twelve co-detections. The diffractive-scintillation
excess relative to the modeled Milky-Way floor is robust to the choice of Galactic
electron-density model among the scattering-calibrated models of the same lineage:
NE2025 \citep{Ocker2026} yields an excess on all six mid-latitude sightlines
($2.6$--$10.7\times$) and NE2001 \citep{CordesLazio2002} confirms it ($6/6$,
$2.2$--$7.3\times$), while YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} reverses it, predicting a floor below the
measured value on five of the six. We attribute this reversal to YMW16's per-sightline
scattering unreliability rather than to an absence of excess: by construction YMW16
incorporates no interstellar-scattering data and derives $\tau$ from an empirical
$\tau$--$\mathrm{DM}$ relation with $\sim\!1$\,dex scatter, whereas NE2025 and NE2001
forward-model scattering from the electron-density-fluctuation field. Because the adopted
NE2025 floor itself carries a factor $\sim\!2$--$3$ per-sightline uncertainty that acts as a
systematic shared across sightlines---and therefore does not average down as $\sqrt{N}$---we
regard only the high-amplitude sightlines ($\sim\!7$--$11\times$) as robust evidence for an
extragalactic scattering screen, with the lowest-amplitude cases ($\sim\!2.6$--$3\times$)
remaining consistent with the floor uncertainty.

\subsubsection{The joint two-band fit}
\label{sec:jointfit}

A single-band scattering fit cannot separate the scattering index $\alpha$ from
the scattering time, because the pulse broadening
$\tau(\nu)=\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,(\nu/1\,\mathrm{GHz})^{-\alpha}$ enters a
single band only through its value at that band's frequency: one band fixes the
product, not the slope. Co-detection breaks this degeneracy. We fit the
CHIME/FRB ($\sim$0.4--0.8\,GHz) and DSA-110 ($\sim$1.4\,GHz) dynamic spectra
\emph{simultaneously} with a shared sightline pair
$(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ and per-telescope pulse parameters (arrival
time $t_0$, intrinsic width $\zeta$, and a residual dispersion
$\delta\mathrm{DM}$). The $\sim$1\,GHz lever arm between the bands measures
$\alpha$ directly: the ratio of the two scattering times pins the slope, while
the shared normalization $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ pins its value at 1\,GHz. With
$\alpha$ held at the Kolmogorov value $4.4$ \citep{Rickett1990}, the two bands
instead return $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ estimates that disagree by up to an
order of magnitude for the same sightline---evidence that $\alpha\neq4.4$ along
these lines of sight.

The shape of the scattering tail---the pulse-broadening function (PBF)---is set
by the geometry of the scattering medium: a single thin screen produces an
exponential tail, while an extended or shallow-spectrum medium produces a heavier
power-law tail (Figure~\ref{fig:pbf_shapes}; \citealp[\S11.2]{Cordes2025}). Fit
band by band, individual bursts can appear to prefer different PBFs in the two
bands---for FRB~20221203A a Kolmogorov power-law improves the CHIME evidence while
DSA-110 rejects any tail heavier than exponential (Figure~\ref{fig:pbf_evidence}),
and a per-band PBF formally beats a single exponential by $\Delta\ln Z\approx+4$.
A single sightline, however, samples one scattering screen, whose PBF \emph{shape}
is fixed by its geometry; only the timescale $\tau$ scales with frequency. A PBF
that switches functional form between $0.6$ and $1.4$\,GHz is therefore
unphysical, and the per-band preference is better read as the fit absorbing
band-specific profile structure---an unmodeled sub-component or intrinsic
asymmetry---into the tail shape. We accordingly adopt a single exponential PBF for
both bands. Re-fitting the full sample under this physical choice moves the
well-constrained scattering indices by $\le0.1$ relative to the per-band fits---so
the simplification costs nothing for the clean sightlines---while a marginal,
poorly-constrained sightline shifts by ${\approx}0.6$ in $\alpha$
(Figure~\ref{fig:alpha_pbf}), confirming that the PBF must be fixed on physical
grounds before a per-sightline index is quoted.

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{pbf_shapes}
  \caption{Pulse-broadening function (PBF) shapes used in the joint fit. A Gaussian
  intrinsic pulse convolved with a thin-screen exponential PBF (green) versus
  power-law-spectrum PBFs with density index $\beta=3.00$ and $\beta=11/3$
  (Kolmogorov). Smaller $\beta$ yields a heavier $t^{-\beta/2}$ tail; $\beta\to4$
  recovers the exponential \citep[\S11.2]{Cordes2025}.}
  \label{fig:pbf_shapes}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure*}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{wilhelm_pbf_evidence}
  \caption{Band-dependent PBF preference for FRB~20221203A. \emph{Left:} change in
  log-evidence relative to the exponential PBF versus power-law index $\beta$ (best
  single-component, per band). CHIME (400--800\,MHz) mildly prefers a Kolmogorov
  tail ($\Delta\ln Z=+6.2$ at $\beta=11/3$); DSA (1.2--1.5\,GHz) rejects
  heavier-than-exponential tails monotonically. \emph{Right:} joint CHIME+DSA
  evidence (C1D1). A \emph{per-band} PBF (CHIME power-law, DSA exponential) beats the
  all-exponential model by $\Delta\ln Z=+4.0$ ($\sim6\sigma$), whereas a global
  power-law loses $84$. Taken at face value this favors a per-band PBF; we argue
  in the text that it is an unphysical artifact and adopt a single exponential PBF.}
  \label{fig:pbf_evidence}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{alpha_pbf_systematic}
  \caption{Effect of the PBF choice on the per-sightline scattering index. For
  each burst the open marker is $\alpha$ under the (unphysical) per-band PBF and
  the filled marker, with $1\sigma$ posterior, is $\alpha$ under the adopted
  single-exponential PBF. The well-constrained single-screen sightlines (green)
  are insensitive to the choice ($|\Delta\alpha|\le0.1$); only a marginal,
  poorly-constrained sightline (orange) moves appreciably (${\approx}0.6$), and
  two sightlines are pinned at the $\alpha=1.5$ prior floor under either PBF
  (grey). The dashed line marks the Kolmogorov value $\alpha=4.4$.}
  \label{fig:alpha_pbf}
\end{figure}

A complementary, model-free check that the recovered index is not corrupted by
unmodelled multiplicity is to resolve each burst's profile \emph{within} the CHIME
band. Splitting the band into four $80\,\mathrm{MHz}$ sub-bands and tracking the
on-pulse width with frequency separates genuine scattering, whose width grows as
$\nu^{-\alpha}$, from frequency-independent intrinsic sub-structure, which does not
(Figure~\ref{fig:chime_subband_compare}); a burst whose sub-band widths fail to
scale is a candidate for hidden sub-components and is flagged before a single-screen
index is quoted.

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{chime_subband_compare}
  \caption{Within-CHIME sub-band profiles for the co-detected FRBs johndoeII and
  wilhelm, each split into four $80\,\mathrm{MHz}$ sub-bands across
  $0.40$--$0.80\,\mathrm{GHz}$. Each panel shows the baseline-subtracted on-pulse
  profile against time, annotated with the model-free rms width $w_{\mathrm{rms}}$
  and the trailing-edge $e$-folding time $w_{\mathrm{tail}}$. A purely scattering
  origin predicts both widths increasing toward lower frequency as $\nu^{-\alpha}$,
  whereas frequency-independent intrinsic sub-structure does not; the empty
  $0.40$--$0.48\,\mathrm{GHz}$ sub-band for wilhelm is a data gap, not a
  non-detection. This profile-shape evolution is the diagnostic used to flag
  unmodelled temporal sub-components before a scattering index is quoted. Generated
  by the \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline.}
  \label{fig:chime_subband_compare}
\end{figure}

A stronger, quantitative version of this check fits the scattering time directly
in each sub-band. We split each band into sub-bands (four across CHIME, three
across DSA-110), collapse each to a one-dimensional profile, and fit a scattered
pulse---a Gaussian intrinsic shape convolved with a one-sided exponential tail,
the exponentially-modified Gaussian---to each sub-band independently, leaving the
scattering time $\tau$ free at every frequency. Because each $\tau_i$ is measured
per sub-band with no power law imposed, the slope of $\tau_i(\nu_i)$ is an estimate
of $\alpha$ that shares neither parametrization nor likelihood with the joint
two-dimensional fit, so its agreement with the joint value is an independent
validation rather than a self-consistency check
(Figure~\ref{fig:subband_tau}). For the well-constrained single-screen
sightlines the sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ trace the joint power law and recover the joint
index to within $1.6\sigma$ (e.g.\ FRB~20240229A, $\alpha=2.40$ versus
$2.35\pm0.03$; FRB~20221203A, $2.56$ versus $2.45\pm0.07$). The check also
localizes the one elevated band $\chi^2$ in our sample: the DSA profile of
FRB~20221203A is imperfectly fit, but its sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ still follow the
joint slope, so the residual reflects unmodeled profile sub-structure rather than a
failure of the single-screen scattering law, and $\alpha$ is unaffected. The method
has a built-in horizon: where a burst is faint and heavily scattered the per-sub-band
fit cannot resolve the broad CHIME tail, and at the top of the DSA band $\tau$ falls
below the time resolution, so the most strongly scattered sightlines (e.g.\
FRB~20220506D) retain only DSA detections over too short a lever to constrain
$\alpha$ on their own---there the joint fit's cross-band lever does work that no
single band can. We therefore use the sub-band reconstruction as a corroborating
cross-check on the well-constrained indices, not as a primary estimator.

\begin{figure*}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{subband_tau_validation_grid}
  \caption{Independent sub-band validation of the joint scattering index. For each
  burst the scattering time $\tau$ is fit directly in four CHIME (blue) and three
  DSA-110 (red) sub-bands with $\tau$ free at every frequency, and compared to the
  joint-fit power law $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,\nu^{-\alpha}$ (black); each panel
  lists the joint $\alpha$ and the slope $\alpha$ of the independent sub-band points.
  \emph{Top:} three well-constrained sightlines whose sub-band $\tau(\nu)$ recover
  the joint index to within $1.6\sigma$. \emph{Bottom:} two sightlines with a mild
  ($\Delta\alpha\approx0.4$) offset traceable to the intrinsic-width--scattering
  degeneracy in a single bright band (FRB~20230325A) and to a low DSA $\tau$
  (FRB~20230307A), and one strongly-scattered sightline (FRB~20220506D) whose CHIME
  tail is unresolved, leaving only DSA detections over too short a lever to constrain
  $\alpha$. Downward triangles are $2\sigma$ upper limits; sub-bands where the
  scattered-pulse model does not describe the profile are dropped rather than shown
  as limits. Generated by the \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline.}
  \label{fig:subband_tau}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Gain-marginalized likelihood}
\label{sec:gainmarg}

The per-channel amplitude of an FRB spectrum is unknown and strongly modulated
by scintillation; carried as a free spectral envelope it leaves correlated
structure in the fit residuals and invalidates a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test.
We therefore marginalize the per-channel gain analytically. With a Gaussian
prior on the gain the per-channel evidence is available in closed form (a
matched-filter likelihood), and the recovered gain absorbs both the burst
spectrum and the scintillation pattern. This removes the spectral and
scintillation structure from the residual, so that the reduced $\chi^2$ and the
lag-1 residual autocorrelation become meaningful quality gates, and it lowers
the sampled dimensionality. We
sample the posterior by nested sampling \citep{Skilling2004}, as implemented in
\texttt{dynesty} \citep{Speagle2020}, and quality-gate each fit on its reduced
$\chi^2$ and residual whiteness, treating $R^2$ and residual normality as
informational.

\subsubsection{Multiple temporal components}
\label{sec:multicomp}

Some bursts comprise more than one temporal sub-component, a common feature of
the resolved FRB population \citep{Hessels2019, Pleunis2021}. An unmodeled
second component is partially absorbed by a single scattered pulse---as a steeper slope
and a larger scattering time---and so biases the inferred $\alpha$. We extend
the likelihood to $N$ temporal components per band, each contributing its own
$(t_0,\zeta)$ while $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ remain shared across both
bands and all components, and we select the number of components per band by
Bayesian evidence. Because the evidence normalization differs between likelihood
variants, the component counts are compared on a single matched grid in which
even the one-component model is evaluated through the multi-component
likelihood, so that evidence differences are meaningful. Component arrival times
within a band are ordered and separated by a data-derived minimum spacing,
keeping the fit identifiable at every prior sample. A clean single-component
co-detection in our sample, for which the joint fit returns
$\alpha=4.48\pm0.04$ with white residuals, validates the method; a
multi-component case is presented in Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}.
% Validating clean case = burst internally labelled "FRB 20230325A" (swap to TNS name).

1	\section{Observations and Sample}
2	\label{sec:obs}
3	
4	Our sample is the twelve fast radio bursts detected simultaneously by CHIME/FRB
5	\citep{CHIMEFRB2018} at $0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz and by DSA-110 \citep{Law2024} at
6	$\sim$1.4\,GHz between 2022 February and 2024 February (MJD $59617$--$60369$).
7	The defining asset of the sample is that each burst is seen twice across a
8	$\sim$1\,GHz frequency baseline, which---as developed in
9	Section~\ref{sec:jointfit}---breaks the degeneracy between the scattering time
10	and its spectral index that defeats any single-band fit. The bursts span a
11	dispersion measure range of $262$--$960$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$; their nicknames, TNS
12	designations, epochs, widths, and time-of-arrival residuals are listed in
13	Table~\ref{tab:toa}. We report all twelve, noting per sightline the level at
14	which each quantity is constrained rather than imposing a single detection
15	threshold.
16	
17	\subsection{Dynamic spectra and reduction}
18	\label{sec:data}
19	
20	For each co-detection we analyze a pair of total-intensity dynamic spectra---one
21	per facility---derived from the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 baseband recordings
22	(24 spectra in total; provenance and array geometry in
23	\texttt{DATA\_SOURCES.md} of the companion \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} archive). Each
24	spectrum is loaded with its frequency axis standardized to ascending order and
25	its per-channel bandpass normalized, so the fitted amplitudes are free of the
26	band-edge rolloff. All fits, quality gates, and figures in this paper are
27	produced by the open \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline (\texttt{pipeline/}), which
28	we reference throughout for reproducibility. We do not catalog the fine
29	baseband morphology of each burst here; the only morphological distinction the
30	analysis depends on---whether a burst resolves into more than one temporal
31	component---is treated where it matters, in the multi-component fit of
32	Section~\ref{sec:multicomp}.
33	
34	\subsection{DSA-110 scattering fits}
35	\label{sec:obs-scatt}
36	
37	The joint CHIME--DSA fit yields a $1.4$\,GHz pulse-broadening time
38	$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ wherever a scattering tail is resolved, measured against
39	the NE2001 Galactic prediction along the sightline. We fit the joint two-band
40	model across the co-detected sample under a single physical (exponential)
41	pulse-broadening function, modeling temporal multiplicity per band
42	(Sections~\ref{sec:results-alpha}, \ref{sec:disc-alpha}). The well-constrained
43	single-screen sightlines return a shared $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ stable
44	to $\le0.1$ in $\alpha$ against the pulse-broadening-function and multiplicity
45	choices; the marginal sightlines---those whose posterior rails or reaches a prior
46	bound---we treat as unconstrained and quote no index rather than report an
47	artifact of the prior. The final per-sightline $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$
48	from this uniform re-fit is reported in Table~\ref{tab:alpha} (eight
49	well-constrained sightlines); the predicted intervening contribution along every
50	sightline---the quantity we can compute without a measurement---is presented in
51	Section~\ref{sec:budget} and Figure~\ref{fig:budget}, and overlaying the measured
52	$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ on that prediction is the principal remaining step for the
53	sample.
54	
55	\subsection{Foreground-galaxy search}
56	\label{sec:obs-fg}
57	
58	To establish which sightlines could carry an intervening dispersion or
59	scattering contribution, we searched each of the twelve fields for foreground
60	galaxies and clusters, cross-matching candidates against the DESI Legacy Imaging
61	Survey DR9 with \citet{Zhou2021} photometric redshifts, DESI DR1 spectroscopic
62	redshifts, the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database, and PS1-STRM \citep{Beck2021}.
63	The search returns 49 candidate systems---34 galaxy halos and 15 clusters---along
64	nine of the twelve sightlines (three fields yield no candidate). For each
65	candidate we record the proper impact parameter (12--280\,kpc for the halos;
66	clusters by $b/R_{500}$) and the best available redshift and its provenance
67	(spectroscopic or photometric); the full catalog is Table~\ref{tab:foreground}.
68	Spectroscopic host redshifts are available for nine of the twelve bursts
69	($z=0.043$--$0.51$), while three (FRB 20230325A, FRB 20240122A, FRB 20230814B) carry only a
70	placeholder redshift and are excluded from any distance-dependent quantity. We measure no host stellar masses; the only masses entering the
71	analysis are the halo-mass proxies of the intervening systems used by the
72	circumgalactic dispersion model (Section~\ref{sec:dm}), whose confidence we flag
73	per system. The verdict logic that classifies each candidate as foreground,
74	background, or inconclusive, and the resulting dispersion budget, follow in
75	Section~\ref{sec:budget}.
76

file.content:
\section{Observations and Sample}
\label{sec:obs}

Our sample is the twelve fast radio bursts detected simultaneously by CHIME/FRB
\citep{CHIMEFRB2018} at $0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz and by DSA-110 \citep{Law2024} at
$\sim$1.4\,GHz between 2022 February and 2024 February (MJD $59617$--$60369$).
The defining asset of the sample is that each burst is seen twice across a
$\sim$1\,GHz frequency baseline, which---as developed in
Section~\ref{sec:jointfit}---breaks the degeneracy between the scattering time
and its spectral index that defeats any single-band fit. The bursts span a
dispersion measure range of $262$--$960$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$; their nicknames, TNS
designations, epochs, widths, and time-of-arrival residuals are listed in
Table~\ref{tab:toa}. We report all twelve, noting per sightline the level at
which each quantity is constrained rather than imposing a single detection
threshold.

\subsection{Dynamic spectra and reduction}
\label{sec:data}

For each co-detection we analyze a pair of total-intensity dynamic spectra---one
per facility---derived from the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 baseband recordings
(24 spectra in total; provenance and array geometry in
\texttt{DATA\_SOURCES.md} of the companion \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} archive). Each
spectrum is loaded with its frequency axis standardized to ascending order and
its per-channel bandpass normalized, so the fitted amplitudes are free of the
band-edge rolloff. All fits, quality gates, and figures in this paper are
produced by the open \texttt{dsa110-FLITS} pipeline (\texttt{pipeline/}), which
we reference throughout for reproducibility. We do not catalog the fine
baseband morphology of each burst here; the only morphological distinction the
analysis depends on---whether a burst resolves into more than one temporal
component---is treated where it matters, in the multi-component fit of
Section~\ref{sec:multicomp}.

\subsection{DSA-110 scattering fits}
\label{sec:obs-scatt}

The joint CHIME--DSA fit yields a $1.4$\,GHz pulse-broadening time
$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ wherever a scattering tail is resolved, measured against
the NE2001 Galactic prediction along the sightline. We fit the joint two-band
model across the co-detected sample under a single physical (exponential)
pulse-broadening function, modeling temporal multiplicity per band
(Sections~\ref{sec:results-alpha}, \ref{sec:disc-alpha}). The well-constrained
single-screen sightlines return a shared $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$ stable
to $\le0.1$ in $\alpha$ against the pulse-broadening-function and multiplicity
choices; the marginal sightlines---those whose posterior rails or reaches a prior
bound---we treat as unconstrained and quote no index rather than report an
artifact of the prior. The final per-sightline $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$
from this uniform re-fit is reported in Table~\ref{tab:alpha} (eight
well-constrained sightlines); the predicted intervening contribution along every
sightline---the quantity we can compute without a measurement---is presented in
Section~\ref{sec:budget} and Figure~\ref{fig:budget}, and overlaying the measured
$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ on that prediction is the principal remaining step for the
sample.

\subsection{Foreground-galaxy search}
\label{sec:obs-fg}

To establish which sightlines could carry an intervening dispersion or
scattering contribution, we searched each of the twelve fields for foreground
galaxies and clusters, cross-matching candidates against the DESI Legacy Imaging
Survey DR9 with \citet{Zhou2021} photometric redshifts, DESI DR1 spectroscopic
redshifts, the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database, and PS1-STRM \citep{Beck2021}.
The search returns 49 candidate systems---34 galaxy halos and 15 clusters---along
nine of the twelve sightlines (three fields yield no candidate). For each
candidate we record the proper impact parameter (12--280\,kpc for the halos;
clusters by $b/R_{500}$) and the best available redshift and its provenance
(spectroscopic or photometric); the full catalog is Table~\ref{tab:foreground}.
Spectroscopic host redshifts are available for nine of the twelve bursts
($z=0.043$--$0.51$), while three (FRB 20230325A, FRB 20240122A, FRB 20230814B) carry only a
placeholder redshift and are excluded from any distance-dependent quantity. We measure no host stellar masses; the only masses entering the
analysis are the halo-mass proxies of the intervening systems used by the
circumgalactic dispersion model (Section~\ref{sec:dm}), whose confidence we flag
per system. The verdict logic that classifies each candidate as foreground,
background, or inconclusive, and the resulting dispersion budget, follow in
Section~\ref{sec:budget}.

content.text: No "grand" terminology exists anywhere — that grep only hit "inteGRANDd". So "four grand galaxies / four grand galaxy clusters" is your framing, not an existing label. The data points to natural candidates but the selection isn't unambiguous. Let me check what galaxy/cluster figures already exist on disk and whether any analysis doc already named the standout systems before I ask.

cd /Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS

rg -il "most significant|dominant interven|standout|four (galax|cluster|halo)|grand" galaxies/ .scratch/ analysis/ docs/ 2>/dev/null | head -20

=== generate_galaxy_plots.main(): what it reads ===
def main():
    # Resolve paths relative to the repo root
    base_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))
    results_dir = os.path.join(base_dir, "results")
    docs_dir = os.path.join(base_dir, "docs")

    # Import targets from config
    sys.path.insert(0, base_dir)
    from galaxies.v2_0.config import TARGETS
    from galaxies.v2_0.plotting import _split_galaxies_clusters

    target_sections = []

    for name, ra_str, dec_str, z_frb in TARGETS:
        csv_path = os.path.join(results_dir, f"{name.lower()}_galaxies.csv")
        if not os.path.exists(csv_path):
            print(f"  {name}: no CSV found, skipping.")
            continue

        df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
        # Drop cluster rows: this NFW/L*-halo dashboard models galaxies only; clusters
        # would mis-render as L* halos. Clusters now live in the on-sky maps (plotting.py).
        df, _clusters = _split_galaxies_clusters(df)
        if df.empty:
            print(f"  {name}: no foreground galaxies (clusters shown in sky maps), skipping.")
            continue

        # Build per-galaxy data
        gal_rows = []
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            z_gal = row["z"]
            impact = row["impact_kpc"]

            if "M_star" in row and not np.isnan(row["M_star"]) and row["M_star"] > 0:
                log_mstar = row["M_star"]
                mass_source = "catalog"
            else:
                phot = estimate_logmstar_from_photometry(row, z_gal)
                if phot is not None:
                    log_mstar, mass_source = phot, "photometric"
                else:
                    # No catalog M* and no usable photometry (e.g. DESI grz
                    # without i-band): assume a typical L* galaxy.
                    log_mstar, mass_source = 10.0, "assumed"

            m_halo = estimate_halo_mass(log_mstar)
            r_vir, r_s, c = get_rvir_and_rs(m_halo, z_gal)
            d_com = cosmo.comoving_distance(z_gal).to(u.Mpc).value

            gal_rows.append(
                {
                    "z_gal": z_gal,
                    "d_com": d_com,
                    "impact": impact,
                    "log_mstar": log_mstar,
                    "mass_source": mass_source,
                    "m_halo": m_halo,
                    "r_vir": r_vir,
                    "r_s": r_s,
                    "c": c,
                }
            )

        if not gal_rows:
            continue

        print(f"  {name}: {len(gal_rows)} galaxies")

        # Generate per-target figures
        fig1, b64_sl = make_sightline_fig(name, z_frb, gal_rows)
        sl_path = os.path.join(results_dir, f"{name.lower()}_sightline.png")

content.text: No stub exists — §5.2 "Intervening Foreground Galaxies and Clusters" is prose + tables only. The task is to add per-system figures + write the prose. Two things genuinely aren't derivable and drive the whole build: which systems count as "grand," and the figure form. The data points to clear candidates; let me confirm before generating.

content.text: Decisions locked: galaxies = the 4 largest DM_int (FRB 20230307A, 20230814B, 20220310F, 20220207C); clusters = 4 closest by b/R500 (all FRB 20230307A field); form = two 2×2 composites (PDF+SVG).
